Authors: Wen Zhang; Pinghua Liu; Lei Zou; Lilin Du; Chonghui Yang; Hua Xiang; Guangyu Huang; Wanpeng Zhou; Jianlong Wang; Qianyu Li; Hao Sun; Guochun Zhao

Journal: Precambrian Research

DOI: 10.1016/j.precamres.2026.108162

Relevance: 0.606 (consider)

The Trans-North China Orogen (TNCO), marking the final amalgamation of the Western and Eastern Blocks of the North China Craton (NCC), provides key insights into Paleoproterozoic crustal evolution and the assembly of the Columbia supercontinent. Here, we investigate garnet-bearing amphibolites derived from mafic volcanic protoliths of the Liyanghe Formation (Zanhuang Group) in the Zanhuang Complex of the central TNCO. Petrography and phase equilibria modeling delineate a clockwise P–T trajectory suggesting a peak high-pressure (HP) amphibolite-facies stage (M2: 1.32–1.63 GPa, 715–750 °C) followed by near-isothermal decompression to lower-pressure amphibolite-facies conditions (M3: 0.61–0.88 GPa, 680–718 °C). Furthermore, high-Ti amphibole compositions and oriented rutile exsolution within garnet provide evidence for a cryptic granulite-facies thermal peak, the diagnostic mineral records of which were likely obliterated by pervasive suprasolidus re-equilibration. Multi-mineral LA-ICP-MS U–Pb geochronology reveals a probable temporal decoupling between peak and cooling stages: metamorphic zircon dating constrains the timing of peak metamorphism to 1870 ± 8 Ma and 1868 ± 8 Ma, whereas cooling phases are consistently younger, with titanite at 1810 ± 16 Ma, rutile at 1799 ± 12 Ma and 1805 ± 6 Ma, and apatite at 1812 ± 31 Ma. Our results demonstrate a prolonged deep crustal residence (∼60 Myr) with negligible cooling (<1 °C/Myr), followed by rapid exhumation at ∼1.81 Ga with a cooling rate of 11.8 - 5.8 + 23.9 °C/Myr. These findings support a Paleoproterozoic final collision model for the TNCO and attribute this rapid ∼1.81 Ga exhumation to lithospheric delamination or slab break-off, marking the definitive collapse of the orogen.

item.title: Fluid exsolution in porphyry-skarn magmas constrained from volatile-bearing minerals in the Late Triassic Saishitang complex, East Kunlun Orogen, China
item.dc:creator: Liang Pan
Jinyang Zhang
Yuanming Pan
Qin Huang
Qilin Wang
Jiaxing Chai
Lanxing Zhang
Zhenbing She
Changqian Ma
Jianwei Li
item.description: Fluid saturation and exsolution play critical roles in forming porphyry-skarn deposits, but the timing and depth of this process during transcrustal magmatic evolution are often difficult to determine. This study uses volatile compositions of amphibole, biotite, and apatite to reconstruct fluid exsolution during magmatic evolution in the Saishitang complex formed from a transcrustal magmatic system. The Saishitang complex is primarily associated with skarn Cu mineralization in the East Kunlun Orogen, western China and comprises a pluton consisting of quartz diorite, granodiorite, and monzogranite intruded by a series of porphyritic dykes of diorite, quartz diorite, granodiorite, and granite. Calculated F and Cl contents for corresponding melts from amphibole, biotite, and apatite of each rock type in the complex, as well as temperatures obtained from the amphibole geothermometer, reveal that an abrupt drop in Cl-H2O concentrations, marking the onset of fluid saturation at 780–900 ℃ in both porphyritic and equigranular rocks. The Al-in-amphibole geobarometer yields pressures of 81–180 MPa for fluid exsolution, corresponding to a paleodepth of 3.0–6.8 km. These results as well as fractional crystallization modeling show that the Saishitang magmatic system was Cl undersaturated at the middle to lower crustal levels before fluid exsolution in the upper crust. These conclusions are different from the recent model for fluid exsolution at the middle or lower crust suggested by melt inclusions. The study demonstrates that Cl and H2O trends can reliably trace fluid exsolution in the Saishitang complex and thus establishes a robust approach for reconstructing magmatic fluid saturation histories through the analysis of volatile-bearing minerals, particularly in settings where direct melt records are absent or compromised. Published: 2026-06-08T15:48:01+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.605 (consider)

Authors: Liang Pan; Jinyang Zhang; Yuanming Pan; Qin Huang; Qilin Wang; Jiaxing Chai; Lanxing Zhang; Zhenbing She; Changqian Ma; Jianwei Li

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

Fluid saturation and exsolution play critical roles in forming porphyry-skarn deposits, but the timing and depth of this process during transcrustal magmatic evolution are often difficult to determine. This study uses volatile compositions of amphibole, biotite, and apatite to reconstruct fluid exsolution during magmatic evolution in the Saishitang complex formed from a transcrustal magmatic system. The Saishitang complex is primarily associated with skarn Cu mineralization in the East Kunlun Orogen, western China and comprises a pluton consisting of quartz diorite, granodiorite, and monzogranite intruded by a series of porphyritic dykes of diorite, quartz diorite, granodiorite, and granite. Calculated F and Cl contents for corresponding melts from amphibole, biotite, and apatite of each rock type in the complex, as well as temperatures obtained from the amphibole geothermometer, reveal that an abrupt drop in Cl-H2O concentrations, marking the onset of fluid saturation at 780–900 ℃ in both porphyritic and equigranular rocks. The Al-in-amphibole geobarometer yields pressures of 81–180 MPa for fluid exsolution, corresponding to a paleodepth of 3.0–6.8 km. These results as well as fractional crystallization modeling show that the Saishitang magmatic system was Cl undersaturated at the middle to lower crustal levels before fluid exsolution in the upper crust. These conclusions are different from the recent model for fluid exsolution at the middle or lower crust suggested by melt inclusions. The study demonstrates that Cl and H2O trends can reliably trace fluid exsolution in the Saishitang complex and thus establishes a robust approach for reconstructing magmatic fluid saturation histories through the analysis of volatile-bearing minerals, particularly in settings where direct melt records are absent or compromised.

item.title: Ages and geochemistry of Cretaceous igneous rocks record the transition from compression to extension in Fujian Province, South China
item.dc:creator: Yuelan Kang
Yuruo Shi
J. Lawford Anderson
Huiyi Sun
item.description: Late Mesozoic magmatism in South China provides critical insights into East Asia continental margin evolution. Cretaceous magmatic rocks in coastal Fujian Province, South China, record the coupled magmatic-tectonic evolution of this area, yet key questions remain regarding magma source transformation, timing of the compressional-extensional transition, and its driving mechanisms. This study focused on Cretaceous magmatic rocks in eastern Fujian, South China, employing zircon U-Pb dating, zircon Hf isotope analyses, and whole-rock analyses. These samples are categorized as undifferentiated I-type granites, highly differentiated I-type granites, and A-type granites. The I-type granites were derived from ancient crustal melting with crust-mantle interaction, while the A-type granites were generated by high-temperature and anhydrous remelting of enriched crustal components, with plagioclase fractionation in an extensional environment. In combination with available geochemical data in this area, magma sources shifted from 130 Ma to 80 Ma due to changing crustal contributions and enhanced fractional crystallization accompanied by melt extraction from the magma chamber. The following dynamic model is proposed: During 130−110 Ma, Paleo-Pacific plate subduction caused compression and thickened the crust, generating I-type granites via crust-mantle hybridization and crystallization differentiation. From 110 Ma to 100 Ma, slab rollback increased the subduction angle, triggering asthenospheric upwelling and lithospheric delamination, shifting stress from compression to extension. Lithospheric thinning dominated the 100−80 Ma interval, during which extensional tectonics facilitated the formation of A-type granites. Published: 2026-06-02T19:01:07+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.604 (consider)

Late Mesozoic magmatism in South China provides critical insights into East Asia continental margin evolution. Cretaceous magmatic rocks in coastal Fujian Province, South China, record the coupled magmatic-tectonic evolution of this area, yet key questions remain regarding magma source transformation, timing of the compressional-extensional transition, and its driving mechanisms. Lithospheric thinning dominated the 100−80 Ma interval, during which extensional tectonics facilitated the formation of A-type granites.

item.title: Global geodynamics during the Archean: A geochemistry perspective on TTGs and mantle-derived rocks from several Cratons worldwide
item.dc:creator: Rodrigo S. Marimon
Chris J. Hawkesworth
Jaana Halla
Henrique Bruno
Monica Heilbron
item.description: Geodynamics in the early Archean has been the subject of intense debate and two predominant schools of thought have emerged. Uniformitarians argue that plate tectonics have dominated the Earth's history, albeit with some temporal differences. Conversely, non-uniformitarians support a distinct geodynamic mode operated in the early Archean, characterized by vertical motions and plume activity, with subordinate intermittent subduction. In this contribution, we use a worldwide database of tonalites-trondhjemites-granodiorites (TTGs) and mafic-ultramafic rocks, coupled with batch melting calculations, to constrain geodynamics in the Archean. TTGs are the dominant felsic igneous/metaigneous components of Archean cratons. Basaltic rocks (averages) were selected from well-constrained modern tectonic settings as sources for batch melting calculations to establish which might produce melts that are similar to Archean TTGs. Th/Nb was used to discriminate which TTGs, from a worldwide database, had source rocks similar to plateau (non-uniformitarian scenario) or island arc (uniformitarian scenario) basalts. Overall Th/Nb increases steadily with time and prior to ~3.2 Ga TTGs yielded lower Th/Nb values, consistent with mostly plateau non-subduction related basalts as sources. Similarly, Th/Nb of Archean mafic-ultramafic rocks increase with time and a predominance of low Th/Nb prior to ~3.0–3.3 Ga indicates a paucity of subduction-related setting, whereas higher Th/Nb after ~3.0–3.3 Ga point to widespread subduction. The data suggest that the Earth transitioned from a geodynamic mode dominated by vertical tectonics and plume activity with subordinate intermittent subduction at ~3.3–3.0 Ga to a regime akin to modern-style plate tectonics, dominated by magmatism in subduction zones. Published: 2026-06-06T23:05:35+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.592 (consider)

Geodynamics in the early Archean has been the subject of intense debate and two predominant schools of thought have emerged. Uniformitarians argue that plate tectonics have dominated the Earth's history, albeit with some temporal differences. Conversely, non-uniformitarians support a distinct geodynamic mode operated in the early Archean, characterized by vertical motions and plume activity, with subordinate intermittent subduction. In this contribution, we use a worldwide database of tonalites-trondhjemites-granodiorites (TTGs) and mafic-ultramafic rocks, coupled with batch melting calculations, to constrain geodynamics in the Archean. TTGs are the dominant felsic igneous/metaigneous components of Archean cratons. Overall Th/Nb increases steadily with time and prior to ~3.2 Ga TTGs yielded lower Th/Nb values, consistent with mostly plateau non-subduction related basalts as sources. Similarly, Th/Nb of Archean mafic-ultramafic rocks increase with time and a predominance of low Th/Nb prior to ~3.0–3.3 Ga indicates a paucity of subduction-related setting, whereas higher Th/Nb after ~3.0–3.3 Ga point to widespread subduction. The data suggest that the Earth transitioned from a geodynamic mode dominated by vertical tectonics and plume activity with subordinate intermittent subduction at ~3.3–3.0 Ga to a regime akin to modern-style plate tectonics, dominated by magmatism in subduction zones.

item.title: Geochemical and Isotopic Constraints on Long‐Lived Source Enrichment and Mantle Evolution in Paleoproterozoic Cratonic Lamprophyres
item.dc:creator: Sourav Naskar
Rohit Pandey
Nils Björn Baumann
N. V. Chalapathi Rao
George Mathew
J. Amal Dev
J. K. Tomson
item.description: Abstract The cratonic lithospheric mantle records complex metasomatic processes and is frequently tapped by alkaline magmatism, offering a unique opportunity to trace the progressive evolution of the mantle. In the present contribution, we investigate a newly identified calc‐alkaline lamprophyre field from the Neoarchean Jonnagiri Schist Belt, Eastern Dharwar Craton, southern India. Petrographic and mineralogical observations reveal reversely zoned amphibole phenocrysts, indicating magma mixing and recharge within a multi‐level magma plumbing system. Amphibole cores, interpreted as antecrysts, crystallized in a deep‐seated magma reservoir (∼20 km) and were later overgrown by rims equilibrated with a more mafic melt at shallower crustal levels (∼7 km), recording a dynamic history of magmatic evolution. 40 Ar/ 39 Ar age (2,200–2,400 Ma) of the lamprophyres cast a significantly distinct Paleoproterozoic calc‐alkaline event that predates the widespread Mesoproterozoic (∼1,100 Ma) alkaline magmatism elsewhere in the craton. Whole‐rock geochemistry characterized by negative Nb‐Ta, Zr‐Hf, and Ti anomalies in the spidergram, suggests derivation from a sub‐continental lithospheric mantle source modified by slab‐derived fluids during the Neoarchean subduction. The Th/Yb (5.3–2.5) and Nb/U (9.4–2.1) trace‐element ratios, and the fractionated LREE/HREE pattern (La N /Yb N : 29–18) of the investigated lamprophyres are indistinguishable from those of lamprophyres from the Superior‐ and Yilgarn‐ Cratons. Near Chondritic εNd i signatures and enriched Archean mantle‐like Sr isotopic signatures imply a short residence time of the generated magma after metasomatic modification. The geodynamic setup of the Jonnagiri schist belt reflects post‐collisional reworking of the subduction‐modified lithosphere, possibly triggered by the slab break‐off and asthenospheric upwelling during the waning period of Neoarchean accretion. Partial melting of this enriched mantle during the Paleoproterozoic (∼2.3 Ga), likely related to Superia supercontinent break‐up, contributed to the polychronous magmatic activity in this domain. The Jonnagiri lamprophyres record the prolonged influence of a subduction‐enriched lithospheric domain tapped episodically during the Neoarchean to Mesoproterozoic. Authors: Sourav Naskar; Rohit Pandey; Nils Björn Baumann; N. V. Chalapathi Rao; George Mathew; J. Amal Dev; J. K. Tomson

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2025gc012773

Relevance: 0.591 (consider)

Abstract The cratonic lithospheric mantle records complex metasomatic processes and is frequently tapped by alkaline magmatism, offering a unique opportunity to trace the progressive evolution of the mantle. In the present contribution, we investigate a newly identified calc‐alkaline lamprophyre field from the Neoarchean Jonnagiri Schist Belt, Eastern Dharwar Craton, southern India. Petrographic and mineralogical observations reveal reversely zoned amphibole phenocrysts, indicating magma mixing and recharge within a multi‐level magma plumbing system. Amphibole cores, interpreted as antecrysts, crystallized in a deep‐seated magma reservoir (∼20 km) and were later overgrown by rims equilibrated with a more mafic melt at shallower crustal levels (∼7 km), recording a dynamic history of magmatic evolution. 40 Ar/ 39 Ar age (2,200–2,400 Ma) of the lamprophyres cast a significantly distinct Paleoproterozoic calc‐alkaline event that predates the widespread Mesoproterozoic (∼1,100 Ma) alkaline magmatism elsewhere in the craton. Whole‐rock geochemistry characterized by negative Nb‐Ta, Zr‐Hf, and Ti anomalies in the spidergram, suggests derivation from a sub‐continental lithospheric mantle source modified by slab‐derived fluids during the Neoarchean subduction. The Th/Yb (5.3–2.5) and Nb/U (9.4–2.1) trace‐element ratios, and the fractionated LREE/HREE pattern (La N /Yb N : 29–18) of the investigated lamprophyres are indistinguishable from those of lamprophyres from the Superior‐ and Yilgarn‐ Cratons. Near Chondritic εNd i signatures and enriched Archean mantle‐like Sr isotopic signatures imply a short residence time of the generated magma after metasomatic modification. The geodynamic setup of the Jonnagiri schist belt reflects post‐collisional reworking of the subduction‐modified lithosphere, possibly triggered by the slab break‐off and asthenospheric upwelling during the waning period of Neoarchean accretion. Partial melting of this enriched mantle during the Paleoproterozoic (∼2.3 Ga), likely related to Superia supercontinent break‐up, contributed to the polychronous magmatic activity in this domain. The Jonnagiri lamprophyres record the prolonged influence of a subduction‐enriched lithospheric domain tapped episodically during the Neoarchean to Mesoproterozoic.

item.title: Independent evolution of Gondwana terranes along its northern margin during early Tethyan initiation
item.dc:creator: Mingjun Zheng
Jun Deng
Yongjun Lu
Huanchao Xu
Jiale Wang
Yingjie Wang
Xianyi Huang
Ke Miao
Xiang Sun
item.description: The late Ediacaran−early Paleozoic (580−420 Ma) represents a critical interval marked by the final Gondwana assembly and initiation of the Tethyan realm. The Lhasa terrane (southwestern China) is commonly regarded as part of the Indo-Australian margin tectonic system, yet its paleogeographic affinity and tectono-magmatic evolution remain debated. Here we present a systematic comparison of geological records from the Lhasa terrane with those from the South Qiangtang, Himalaya, and Sibumasu terranes as well as the Iran-Anatolia region. Our results reveal significant differences in tectono-magmatic evolution among terranes along the northern margin of Gondwana. The Lhasa terrane records a prolonged subduction-related history from 580 to 480 Ma, comparable to that of the Iran-Anatolia region, including low-angle subduction at 570−525 Ma, slab rollback at 525−510 Ma, and terrane accretion accompanied by slab break-off at 510−480 Ma. In contrast, terranes along the Indo-Australian margin record a coherent two-stage evolution, with 550−520 Ma ocean-island basalt−like magmatism related to the postcollisional extension stage of the Kuunga-Pinjarra orogen, followed by 500−420 Ma arc-backarc magmatism linked to Proto-Tethyan subduction. These contrasts demonstrate that the Lhasa terrane was not part of the Indo-Australian margin but evolved independently along the African margin of Gondwana, an interpretation further supported by metamorphic and detrital-zircon evidence. Our results refine the geodynamic framework of early Tethyan evolution, provide new constraints on the paleogeographic reconstruction of East Asian blocks, and offer insight into the spatial heterogeneity of Phanerozoic tin mineralization. The late Ediacaran−early Paleozoic (580−420 Ma) represents a critical interval marked by the final Gondwana assembly and initiation of the Tethyan realm. The Lhasa terrane (southwestern China) is commonly regarded as part of the Indo-Australian margin tectonic system, yet its paleogeographic affinity and tectono-magmatic evolution remain debated. Here we present a systematic comparison of geological records from the Lhasa terrane with those from the South Qiangtang, Himalaya, and Sibumasu terranes as well as the Iran-Anatolia region. Our results reveal significant differences in tectono-magmatic evolution among terranes along the northern margin of Gondwana. The Lhasa terrane records a prolonged subduction-related history from 580 to 480 Ma, comparable to that of the Iran-Anatolia region, including low-angle subduction at 570−525 Ma, slab rollback at 525−510 Ma, and terrane accretion accompanied by slab break-off at 510−480 Ma. In contrast, terranes along the Indo-Australian margin record a coherent two-stage evolution, with 550−520 Ma ocean-island basalt−like magmatism related to the postcollisional extension stage of the Kuunga-Pinjarra orogen, followed by 500−420 Ma arc-backarc magmatism linked to Proto-Tethyan subduction. These contrasts demonstrate that the Lhasa terrane was not part of the Indo-Australian margin but evolved independently along the African margin of Gondwana, an interpretation further supported by metamorphic and detrital-zircon evidence. Our results refine the geodynamic framework of early Tethyan evolution, provide new constraints on the paleogeographic reconstruction of East Asian blocks, and offer insight into the spatial heterogeneity of Phanerozoic tin mineralization.

item.title: Mineralization characteristics and controlling factors of key metals of Re, Se, and Te in the Bainaimiao Cu-Au-Mo deposit, northern margin of North China Craton
item.dc:creator: Zhenhua Zhou
Renyu Liang
Qing Zhang
Cheng Zhang
Hongwei Yuan
item.description: Porphyry copper deposits are the most important source of critical minerals such as Re, Se, and Te. The Bainaimiao Cu-Au-Mo deposit is located in the eastern segment of the Central Asian Orogenic Belt (CAOB). The northern ore section exhibits porphyry-type alteration and mineralization characteristics, while the ore bodies in the southern ore section mainly occur as stratiform-like layers within the country-rock greenschist. There has long been debate regarding the genetic type of the Bainaimiao deposit. Through in-situ component analysis of different types of sulfides such as pyrite and chalcopyrite, combining with previous data and literatures, we propose that the Bainaimiao belongs to a porphyry deposit modified by later orogenic processes. The high Re content in molybdenite from the Bainaimiao deposit (109–1051 ppm, average of 627 ppm) may be controlled by factors likely its relatively low oxygen fugacity and the incorporation of terrigenous sedimentary components in the magma source. Chalcopyrite from different ore sections have high Se contents, averaging ca. 100 ppm, indicating that chalcopyrite is the primary host mineral for Se. The enrichment of Se is mainly achieved through coupled substitution between S2⁻ and Se2⁻. Tellurium concentrations are relatively low, primarily entering the chalcopyrite lattice as bismuth-tellurium mineral inclusions, which may be related to crustal contamination or the lack of competitive sulfides during Cu mineralization. Porphyry copper deposits in the CAOB have significant potential for mineralization of dispersed elements such as Re, Se, and Te. Future efforts should focus on study of mineralization processes and the comprehensive utilization and recovery of these key metal elements. Published: 2026-06-08T16:26:46+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.587 (consider)

Porphyry copper deposits are the most important source of critical minerals such as Re, Se, and Te. The Bainaimiao Cu-Au-Mo deposit is located in the eastern segment of the Central Asian Orogenic Belt (CAOB). The northern ore section exhibits porphyry-type alteration and mineralization characteristics, while the ore bodies in the southern ore section mainly occur as stratiform-like layers within the country-rock greenschist. Through in-situ component analysis of different types of sulfides such as pyrite and chalcopyrite, combining with previous data and literatures, we propose that the Bainaimiao belongs to a porphyry deposit modified by later orogenic processes. The high Re content in molybdenite from the Bainaimiao deposit (109–1051 ppm, average of 627 ppm) may be controlled by factors likely its relatively low oxygen fugacity and the incorporation of terrigenous sedimentary components in the magma source. Chalcopyrite from different ore sections have high Se contents, averaging ca. 100 ppm, indicating that chalcopyrite is the primary host mineral for Se. The enrichment of Se is mainly achieved through coupled substitution between S2⁻ and Se2⁻. Tellurium concentrations are relatively low, primarily entering the chalcopyrite lattice as bismuth-tellurium mineral inclusions, which may be related to crustal contamination or the lack of competitive sulfides during Cu mineralization. Porphyry copper deposits in the CAOB have significant potential for mineralization of dispersed elements such as Re, Se, and Te. Future efforts should focus on study of mineralization processes and the comprehensive utilization and recovery of these key metal elements.

item.title: When tholeiitic magma mimic alkaline magma: Formation of Na-Ca amphiboles and aenigmatite driven by fluid contamination
item.dc:creator: Alban Cheviet
Christophe Galerne
Martine Buatier
Wolfgang Bach
Anette Meixner
Flavien Choulet
item.description: The emplacement of tholeiitic sills into unconsolidated, volatile-rich sediments provides a unique context to investigate magma–fluid–sediment interactions and their effects on crystallisation. We present petrographic, geochemical, isotopic, and thermodynamic evidence from the ~75 m thick sill at Site U1546 of IODP Expedition 385 in the Guaymas Basin. The sill displays a heterogeneous internal architecture composed of basaltic margins enriched in K₂O and biotite, a doleritic core representative of the primary tholeiitic magma, and coarse-grained gabbroic domains enriched in Na₂O and containing amphibole and aenigmatite. The rocks that compose the pseudogabbroic domains display a texture similar to that gabbro, but their origin is unclear. For this reason, the term “pseudogabbro” will be used throughout the text to refer to them. Geochemical and isotopic data indicate that all lithologies originate from the same parental magma, but their mineralogical differences reflect local contamination and hydration processes. The basaltic margins are contaminated by the infiltration of K-rich metamorphic fluids generated in the contact aureoles, whereas the pseudogabbros formed through fractional crystallisation of a hydrated residual melt of highly alkaline composition, with amphibole and aenigmatite stabilised under variable redox conditions. Thermodynamic models support a two-step crystallisation history involving (1) equilibrium crystallisation of the primary magma, followed by (2) hydration of an incipient mush by infiltration of sediment pore waters, accompanied by fluctuations in fO₂ and late fluid exsolution. These processes illustrate how tholeiitic magmas emplaced in sedimentary basins can mimic alkaline systems through fluid-driven contamination, resulting in the formation of mineralogical assemblages atypical of tholeiitic intrusions. Such mechanisms are critical for understanding volatile transfer, mineral diversity, and redox evolution in shallow magmatic systems. Published: 2026-06-06T03:51:08+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.582 (consider)

Authors: Alban Cheviet; Christophe Galerne; Martine Buatier; Wolfgang Bach; Anette Meixner; Flavien Choulet

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108662

Relevance: 0.582 (consider)

The emplacement of tholeiitic sills into unconsolidated, volatile-rich sediments provides a unique context to investigate magma–fluid–sediment interactions and their effects on crystallisation. We present petrographic, geochemical, isotopic, and thermodynamic evidence from the ~75 m thick sill at Site U1546 of IODP Expedition 385 in the Guaymas Basin. The sill displays a heterogeneous internal architecture composed of basaltic margins enriched in K₂O and biotite, a doleritic core representative of the primary tholeiitic magma, and coarse-grained gabbroic domains enriched in Na₂O and containing amphibole and aenigmatite. The rocks that compose the pseudogabbroic domains display a texture similar to that gabbro, but their origin is unclear. For this reason, the term “pseudogabbro” will be used throughout the text to refer to them. Geochemical and isotopic data indicate that all lithologies originate from the same parental magma, but their mineralogical differences reflect local contamination and hydration processes. The basaltic margins are contaminated by the infiltration of K-rich metamorphic fluids generated in the contact aureoles, whereas the pseudogabbros formed through fractional crystallisation of a hydrated residual melt of highly alkaline composition, with amphibole and aenigmatite stabilised under variable redox conditions. Thermodynamic models support a two-step crystallisation history involving (1) equilibrium crystallisation of the primary magma, followed by (2) hydration of an incipient mush by infiltration of sediment pore waters, accompanied by fluctuations in fO₂ and late fluid exsolution. These processes illustrate how tholeiitic magmas emplaced in sedimentary basins can mimic alkaline systems through fluid-driven contamination, resulting in the formation of mineralogical assemblages atypical of tholeiitic intrusions. Such mechanisms are critical for understanding volatile transfer, mineral diversity, and redox evolution in shallow magmatic systems.

item.title: Pre-main-ore fluid pulses and fluid pathway preparation for Mo enrichment in the Rongga porphyry Mo deposit, Tibet, China
item.dc:creator: Jie Zhang
Bo Peng
Fengqin Ran
Liqiang Wang
Bin Liu
Baolong Li
Kelin Huang
item.description: Porphyry Mo deposits commonly form through multistage magmatic–hydrothermal processes, but the role of pre-main-ore fluid pulses in Mo pre-enrichment and fluid pathway preparation remains poorly constrained. The Rongga deposit is the first recognized porphyry Mo system in the otherwise Cu–Au-dominated Bangong–Nujiang metallogenic belt, Tibet. Its ore-related magmas were derived from a relatively Mo-poor juvenile lower-crust. Here, we combine refined quartz-vein classification with pyrite LA-ICP-MS spot analyses, elemental mapping and unsupervised clustering to evaluate mineral-scale records of fluid influx and Mo enrichment. The vein system includes early A quartz–pyrite veins, transitional AB quartz–pyrite–molybdenite veins, main-stage B quartz–molybdenite veins and late D quartz–pyrite ± polymetallic sulfide veins. Pyrite trace-element data show a stage-related shift from Co–Ni–As-rich A-vein pyrite to mixed Co–Ni–As and Cu–Ag–Zn–Mn–Mo signatures in AB-vein pyrite, Cu–Ag–Zn–Mn–Mo-enriched B-vein pyrite and Pb–Bi–Te-rich D-vein pyrite. AB-vein pyrite occupies an intermediate but distinguishable position between A- and B-vein pyrite. Mo statistics show that B-vein pyrite has the highest median Mo content, whereas AB-vein pyrite records the highest maximum and mean Mo contents. Elemental maps and time-resolved signals further show that Mo-rich domains in AB-vein pyrite occur in both internal and rim/fracture-related domains and reflect mixed Mo-bearing components, including submicroscopic phases and fracture-related fillings, rather than purely lattice-bound Mo in pyrite. These results indicate that Mo enrichment at Rongga was not restricted to the main B-vein stage. We suggest that episodic AB-stage fluid influx promoted localized Mo pre-enrichment and reactivation of earlier vein structures, thereby preparing favorable sites for focused molybdenite precipitation during B-vein formation. Published: 2026-06-06T03:54:11+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.580 (consider)

Elemental maps and time-resolved signals further show that Mo-rich domains in AB-vein pyrite occur in both internal and rim/fracture-related domains and reflect mixed Mo-bearing components, including submicroscopic phases and fracture-related fillings, rather than purely lattice-bound Mo in pyrite. item.title: Late Triassic–Early Jurassic I-type leucogranites and quartz diorites from the Kaçkar Batholith (Eastern Pontides, NE Türkiye): Implications for the generation of highly evolved felsic magmas at convergent plate boundaries
item.dc:creator: Yener Eyuboglu
item.description: This study examines Late Triassic–Early Jurassic I-type leucogranites and quartz diorites from the Kaçkar Batholith (NE Türkiye) to constrain the origin of highly evolved felsic magmas at convergent plate boundaries. Zircon UPb ages indicate crystallization of the Yedigöl quartz diorites and Sırakonaklar leucogranites at ~210–207 Ma, whereas the Altıparmak leucogranites record a younger felsic event at ~192 Ma, documenting episodic magmatism across the Triassic–Jurassic transition. The quartz diorites are calc-alkaline and metaluminous, displaying positive zircon Ce anomalies without Eu depletion, consistent with oxidized, hydrous, mantle-influenced magmas and limited plagioclase fractionation. In contrast, the leucogranites are evolved, weakly peraluminous, and characterized by very low Sr contents and pronounced negative Eu anomalies in both whole-rock and zircon chemistry, reflecting strong feldspar-controlled internal differentiation. The leucogranites include perthite- and antiperthite-bearing varieties, recording contrasting KNa feldspar systematics during magma evolution. All lithologies display mantle-influenced Sr–Nd–Pb and zircon LuHf isotopic signatures consistent with an I-type, juvenile-dominated origin, excluding derivation from metasedimentary sources. Geochemical constraints indicate that the leucogranites did not form through simple fractional crystallization of quartz diorite magmas, but instead originated from feldspar-retentive partial melting of meta-igneous lower crust, driven by sustained thermal input from mantle-influenced magmatism within a vertically coupled arc system and followed by feldspar-dominated differentiation. Enrichment in Nb, Ta, Th, and U reflects magmatic fertility and late-stage accessory mineral concentration rather than economically significant rare-metal mineralization. Overall, the results highlight episodic felsic magma generation above a south-dipping subduction zone during Late Triassic–Early Jurassic. Published: 2026-06-05T15:37:35+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.576 (consider)

This study examines Late Triassic–Early Jurassic I-type leucogranites and quartz diorites from the Kaçkar Batholith (NE Türkiye) to constrain the origin of highly evolved felsic magmas at convergent plate boundaries. Zircon UPb ages indicate crystallization of the Yedigöl quartz diorites and Sırakonaklar leucogranites at ~210–207 Ma, whereas the Altıparmak leucogranites record a younger felsic event at ~192 Ma, documenting episodic magmatism across the Triassic–Jurassic transition. The quartz diorites are calc-alkaline and metaluminous, displaying positive zircon Ce anomalies without Eu depletion, consistent with oxidized, hydrous, mantle-influenced magmas and limited plagioclase fractionation. In contrast, the leucogranites are evolved, weakly peraluminous, and characterized by very low Sr contents and pronounced negative Eu anomalies in both whole-rock and zircon chemistry, reflecting strong feldspar-controlled internal differentiation. The leucogranites include perthite- and antiperthite-bearing varieties, recording contrasting KNa feldspar systematics during magma evolution. All lithologies display mantle-influenced Sr–Nd–Pb and zircon LuHf isotopic signatures consistent with an I-type, juvenile-dominated origin, excluding derivation from metasedimentary sources. Geochemical constraints indicate that the leucogranites did not form through simple fractional crystallization of quartz diorite magmas, but instead originated from feldspar-retentive partial melting of meta-igneous lower crust, driven by sustained thermal input from mantle-influenced magmatism within a vertically coupled arc system and followed by feldspar-dominated differentiation. Enrichment in Nb, Ta, Th, and U reflects magmatic fertility and late-stage accessory mineral concentration rather than economically significant rare-metal mineralization. Overall, the results highlight episodic felsic magma generation above a south-dipping subduction zone during Late Triassic–Early Jurassic.

item.title: Geochemical Constraints on Spatial‐Temporal Distribution and Origin of Anomalous Mantle in the Western Pacific Basins
item.dc:creator: Guo‐Liang Zhang
Han‐Tao Chi
Ji Zhang
Feng Xu
item.description: Abstract Seafloor basalt samples recovered from the Caroline Basin (CB), the Parece Vela Basin (PVB), and the Western Philippine Sea Plate (WPSP) are analyzed for whole‐rock major and trace elements (including H 2 O), Sr‐Nd‐Pb‐Hf isotopes and 40 Ar‐ 39 Ar age‐dating. Two geochemically distinct mantle domains (I and II) are identified for the first time in these marginal basins and their spatio‐temporal distribution and origin are discussed. The Mantle Domain I (PVB and Ayu Trough basalts) has normal mantle‐like H 2 O/Ce ratios and TiO 2 /V ratios, whereas the Mantle Domain II (WPSP and CB basalts, and Mariana fore‐arc basalts) has distinctly lower TiO 2 /V, distinctly more radiogenic Sr and Pb isotopes and higher H 2 O/Ce ratios. The normal mantle‐like H 2 O/Ce of Mantle Domain I indicates negligible influence from subducted components. We provide evidences that the Mantle Domain II extends to the Pacific large low shear velocity province (LLSVP) based plate tectonic reconstruction, and such an Indian‐type mantle ubiquitously exists beneath the Mesozoic Pacific Plate. Our geochemistry results indicate that the giant mantle plumes in LLSVP have played a key role in transporting lower mantle depleted and H 2 O‐rich components to the upper mantle. Published: 2026-06-03T16:44:21+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.574 (consider)

The normal mantle‐like H 2 O/Ce of Mantle Domain I indicates negligible influence from subducted components. Our geochemistry results indicate that the giant mantle plumes in LLSVP have played a key role in transporting lower mantle depleted and H 2 O‐rich components to the upper mantle.

item.title: Fluid pathways, geochemical zonation, and exploration of Carlin-type gold deposits, Youjiang Basin, China: insights from the Lannigou 3D geochemical system
item.dc:creator: Shengtao Cao
Qinping Tan
Zhuojun Xie
Yong Xia
Jianzhong Liu
Yongzhang Zhou
Lujing Zheng
Yumin Lu
Kelin Zhou
Tingxian Ren
Wei Gao
Jingdan Xiao
Ruizhong Hu
item.description: The Youjiang Basin in southwestern China is the world’s second-largest province of Carlin-type gold deposits, yet its exploration potential remains underexplored compared to its counterpart in Nevada, USA. A primary challenge has been the lack of robust, data-driven metallogenic models to guide the search for deeper, concealed orebodies. This study addresses this critical knowledge gap by conducting a comprehensive geochemical investigation of the Lannigou deposit, the largest fault-controlled Carlin-type gold deposit in the basin. In this work, we integrated large, spatially matched geochemical datasets from deep drill-core rock (n = 449) and surface soil (n = 2,280) samples. Through comprehensive statistical analyses, mass transfer calculations, and innovative three-dimensional spatial visualization, we successfully (1) identified distinct ore-related (Au-As-Hg-Sb-Ag-Tl-W-Mo) and diagenetic (Bi, Co, Cu, Ni, Zn, Pb) elemental suites, delineating their controlling geological mechanisms; (2) established a robust vertical geochemical zonation pattern (Mo → Tl → Sb → W → As → Au → Ag → Hg), which serves as a powerful vector for assessing the erosional level of mineralizing systems; and (3) revealed the critical role of a deep-seated, bedding-parallel detachment fault (F1) as a master conduit, channeling ore-forming fluids from basement structures into shallower, ore-hosting reverse faults. Based on these findings, we propose a new metallogenic model wherein ore-forming fluids ascended from depth, migrated laterally along this major detachment fault, and subsequently precipitated gold within structurally favorable traps, such as secondary reverse faults on anticlinal limbs. Our metallogenic model and the identified geochemical vectors offer robust and actionable criteria for targeting concealed Carlin-type orebodies in the Youjiang Basin and analogous terranes worldwide. Published: 2026-06-04T04:14:34+00:00 Venue: Mineralium Deposita Score: 0.574 (consider)

Relevance: 0.574 (consider)

The Youjiang Basin in southwestern China is the world’s second-largest province of Carlin-type gold deposits, yet its exploration potential remains underexplored compared to its counterpart in Nevada, USA. A primary challenge has been the lack of robust, data-driven metallogenic models to guide the search for deeper, concealed orebodies. This study addresses this critical knowledge gap by conducting a comprehensive geochemical investigation of the Lannigou deposit, the largest fault-controlled Carlin-type gold deposit in the basin. In this work, we integrated large, spatially matched geochemical datasets from deep drill-core rock (n = 449) and surface soil (n = 2,280) samples. Through comprehensive statistical analyses, mass transfer calculations, and innovative three-dimensional spatial visualization, we successfully (1) identified distinct ore-related (Au-As-Hg-Sb-Ag-Tl-W-Mo) and diagenetic (Bi, Co, Cu, Ni, Zn, Pb) elemental suites, delineating their controlling geological mechanisms; (2) established a robust vertical geochemical zonation pattern (Mo → Tl → Sb → W → As → Au → Ag → Hg), which serves as a powerful vector for assessing the erosional level of mineralizing systems; and (3) revealed the critical role of a deep-seated, bedding-parallel detachment fault (F1) as a master conduit, channeling ore-forming fluids from basement structures into shallower, ore-hosting reverse faults. Based on these findings, we propose a new metallogenic model wherein ore-forming fluids ascended from depth, migrated laterally along this major detachment fault, and subsequently precipitated gold within structurally favorable traps, such as secondary reverse faults on anticlinal limbs. Our metallogenic model and the identified geochemical vectors offer robust and actionable criteria for targeting concealed Carlin-type orebodies in the Youjiang Basin and analogous terranes worldwide.

item.title: Initiation of the Gangdese Retroarc Fold‐and‐Thrust Belt in Southern Tibet: Evidence From New Geochronologic Data of the Late Cretaceous Shexing Formation
item.dc:creator: Jiawei Zhang
Jingen Dai
Huiping Zhang
Cristina Persano
Wenjun Bi
Chengshan Wang
Xinyu Qian
Jianzhang Pang
Ying Wang
item.description: Abstract The initial exhumation of the fold‐and‐thrust belt in the Gangdese retroarc provides key insights into the development of an Andean‐type continental margin in southern Tibet. Here, we present new zircon U‐Pb geochronology, low‐temperature thermochronology and balanced cross section from the Late Cretaceous retroarc and forearc basins to reconstruct the pre‐collisional exhumation history of the Gangdese fold‐and‐thrust belt. Interbedded andesite and tuffite layers from the upper Shexing Formation in the retroarc basin yield weighted mean U‐Pb ages of 85.5 ± 4.4 and 84.6 ± 0.9 Ma, providing refined constraints on the depositional age. Thermochronologic data from the retroarc and forearc basins record Late Cretaceous‐Cenozoic cooling, with inverse thermal modeling indicating the onset of rapid retroarc exhumation at ∼78–70 Ma, shortly after deposition. This timing likely corresponds to the onset of crustal shortening in the Gangdese retroarc, with a minimum estimate of ∼67% as constrained by balanced cross section restoration. This earliest exhumation signal is also recognized in the forearc and north‐central Lhasa terrane, coinciding with a magmatic lull in the Gangdese arc and northward migration of magmatism. Collectively, we interpret that shallow or flat subduction of the Neo‐Tethyan oceanic lithosphere may have provided the primary geodynamic forcing, whereas surface erosion modulated the mechanical state of the orogenic wedge, jointly promoting Late Cretaceous crustal shortening. Abstract The initial exhumation of the fold‐and‐thrust belt in the Gangdese retroarc provides key insights into the development of an Andean‐type continental margin in southern Tibet. Here, we present new zircon U‐Pb geochronology, low‐temperature thermochronology and balanced cross section from the Late Cretaceous retroarc and forearc basins to reconstruct the pre‐collisional exhumation history of the Gangdese fold‐and‐thrust belt. Interbedded andesite and tuffite layers from the upper Shexing Formation in the retroarc basin yield weighted mean U‐Pb ages of 85.5 ± 4.4 and 84.6 ± 0.9 Ma, providing refined constraints on the depositional age. Thermochronologic data from the retroarc and forearc basins record Late Cretaceous‐Cenozoic cooling, with inverse thermal modeling indicating the onset of rapid retroarc exhumation at ∼78–70 Ma, shortly after deposition. This timing likely corresponds to the onset of crustal shortening in the Gangdese retroarc, with a minimum estimate of ∼67% as constrained by balanced cross section restoration. This earliest exhumation signal is also recognized in the forearc and north‐central Lhasa terrane, coinciding with a magmatic lull in the Gangdese arc and northward migration of magmatism. Collectively, we interpret that shallow or flat subduction of the Neo‐Tethyan oceanic lithosphere may have provided the primary geodynamic forcing, whereas surface erosion modulated the mechanical state of the orogenic wedge, jointly promoting Late Cretaceous crustal shortening.

item.title: Heavy silicon isotope and mass-independent mercury isotope fractionation signals of Archean TTGs: Insights into the recycling of the silicified oceanic crust in the source region
item.dc:creator: Xiao Hu
Wenbin Zhu
Haizhen Wei
Runsheng Yin
Yongxiang Li
Yiwei Rong
Di Chen
item.description: Tonalite-trondhjemite-granodiorite (TTG), the main constituent of the Archean Earth's continental crust, is widely believed to originate from the partial melting of hydrated metamorphic basalts. However, the tectonic setting of the TTG's origin and thus the Archean Earth evolution remains poorly understood. In this study, we performed silicon (Si) isotope, mercury (Hg) isotope, and geochemical data analysis on 3.06 Ga to 2.5 Ga TTG samples from the Dunhuang Block, at the eastern margin of the Tarim Craton, to investigate their protolith composition and the formation environment. The δ30Si values of quartz in these Dunhuang TTG samples range from +0.05 ± 0.07‰ (2SD) to +0.25 ± 0.08‰ (2SD). These consistently heavy quartz Si isotope compositions may indicate that the silicified oceanic crust was incorporated into their protoliths. The Δ199Hg values of the 3.06–2.7 Ga Dunhuang TTG samples range from −0.05‰ to +0.09‰. In contrast, the 2.6–2.5 Ga samples exhibit a wider range (−0.11‰ to +0.14‰), indicating that the younger Dunhuang TTG protolith composition were no longer homogeneous and incorporated supracrustal materials. Coupled SiHg isotopes suggest that supracrustal recycling was a key process in the generation of the Dunhuang TTGs, and that the protolith composition of the 2.6–2.5 Ga Dunhuang TTGs experienced a change compared to the earlier Dunhuang TTG samples. Combined with phase equilibrium modeling and geochemical data, we speculate that the subduction model may represent a reasonable explanation for the formation environment of 2.6–2.5 Ga Dunhuang TTGs, although non-subduction models cannot be ruled out. Published: 2026-06-05T15:36:05+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.571 (consider)

Journal: Chemical Geology

Relevance: 0.571 (consider)

Tonalite-trondhjemite-granodiorite (TTG), the main constituent of the Archean Earth's continental crust, is widely believed to originate from the partial melting of hydrated metamorphic basalts. However, the tectonic setting of the TTG's origin and thus the Archean Earth evolution remains poorly understood. In this study, we performed silicon (Si) isotope, mercury (Hg) isotope, and geochemical data analysis on 3.06 Ga to 2.5 Ga TTG samples from the Dunhuang Block, at the eastern margin of the Tarim Craton, to investigate their protolith composition and the formation environment. The δ30Si values of quartz in these Dunhuang TTG samples range from +0.05 ± 0.07‰ (2SD) to +0.25 ± 0.08‰ (2SD). These consistently heavy quartz Si isotope compositions may indicate that the silicified oceanic crust was incorporated into their protoliths. The Δ199Hg values of the 3.06–2.7 Ga Dunhuang TTG samples range from −0.05‰ to +0.09‰. In contrast, the 2.6–2.5 Ga samples exhibit a wider range (−0.11‰ to +0.14‰), indicating that the younger Dunhuang TTG protolith composition were no longer homogeneous and incorporated supracrustal materials. Coupled SiHg isotopes suggest that supracrustal recycling was a key process in the generation of the Dunhuang TTGs, and that the protolith composition of the 2.6–2.5 Ga Dunhuang TTGs experienced a change compared to the earlier Dunhuang TTG samples. Combined with phase equilibrium modeling and geochemical data, we speculate that the subduction model may represent a reasonable explanation for the formation environment of 2.6–2.5 Ga Dunhuang TTGs, although non-subduction models cannot be ruled out.

item.title: Spatial-temporal distribution of late Cenozoic intraplate basalts in the Leiqiong area, South China: Insights into the distinct roles of mantle plume and continental lithosphere during plume head emplacement
item.dc:creator: Guo-Hong Sun
Xiao-Long Huang
Fan Yang
Yang Yu
Sheng-Zhu Zhu
item.description: The emplacement of plume head is a critical aspect of mantle plume theory; however, the respective roles of the plume and lithosphere during the emplacement remain insufficiently understood. The late Cenozoic Hainan mantle plume, inferred to remain in the plume head stage, preserves records of its emplacement processes. This study presents comprehensive geochemical data, including major and trace elements, as well as Sr–Nd–Pb–Hf isotopes, for late Cenozoic basalts from the northern Leiqiong area (including Leizhou Peninsula, northern Hainan Island, and Weizhou Island), which are associated with the Hainan plume. Based on their spatial-temporal distribution, these basalts are categorized into early and late stages. Early-stage basalts, restricted to eastern Leiqiong area, show positive ΔεHf values (0.51–2.34) and consistently low Ce/Pb (7.22–18.24) and Nb/Th (6.78–10.52) ratios, suggesting a significant contribution from recycled sedimentary components. Late-stage basalts, distributed across the northern Leiqiong area, are subdivided into two groups based on their ΔεHf values. The eastern late-stage basalts display positive ΔεHf values with overall low Ce/Pb and Nb/Th ratios, whereas the western late-stage basalts show negative ΔεHf values (−1.71 to −0.01) with overall higher Ce/Pb (14.59–31.78) and Nb/Th (10.64–12.77) ratios, indicating a substantial contribution of recycled oceanic crust in their mantle source. The spatial-temporal distribution and geochemical variations of the Leiqiong basalts highlight distinct roles of the plume and continental lithosphere during plume head emplacement. The lithosphere architecture strongly controls the ascent and emplacement of the plume head because the plume lacks sufficient vigor, owing to the impediment of significant stagnant slabs in the mantle transition zone beneath the Leiqiong area. Thus, the Hainan mantle plume initially ascended through a lithospheric weak zone in eastern Leiqiong area and subsequently propagated laterally across the northern Leiqiong area due to lateral variations in lithospheric architecture. The mantle sources of both stages were influenced by the Hainan plume during its ascent and lateral propagation. As the plume expanded, fusible recycled sedimentary components accumulated beneath the lithospheric weak zone, while the surrounding mantle was depleted due to tectonic suction toward the central region. Consequently, late-stage basalts in northwestern Leiqiong primarily record a signature of recycled oceanic crust introduced by the plume. Therefore, the plume governs the compositional evolution of the mantle sources during its propagation. Published: 2026-06-07T14:25:32+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.567 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Guo-Hong Sun; Xiao-Long Huang; Fan Yang; Yang Yu; Sheng-Zhu Zhu

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108664

Relevance: 0.567 (consider)

The emplacement of plume head is a critical aspect of mantle plume theory; however, the respective roles of the plume and lithosphere during the emplacement remain insufficiently understood. The late Cenozoic Hainan mantle plume, inferred to remain in the plume head stage, preserves records of its emplacement processes. This study presents comprehensive geochemical data, including major and trace elements, as well as Sr–Nd–Pb–Hf isotopes, for late Cenozoic basalts from the northern Leiqiong area (including Leizhou Peninsula, northern Hainan Island, and Weizhou Island), which are associated with the Hainan plume. Based on their spatial-temporal distribution, these basalts are categorized into early and late stages. Early-stage basalts, restricted to eastern Leiqiong area, show positive ΔεHf values (0.51–2.34) and consistently low Ce/Pb (7.22–18.24) and Nb/Th (6.78–10.52) ratios, suggesting a significant contribution from recycled sedimentary components. Late-stage basalts, distributed across the northern Leiqiong area, are subdivided into two groups based on their ΔεHf values. The eastern late-stage basalts display positive ΔεHf values with overall low Ce/Pb and Nb/Th ratios, whereas the western late-stage basalts show negative ΔεHf values (−1.71 to −0.01) with overall higher Ce/Pb (14.59–31.78) and Nb/Th (10.64–12.77) ratios, indicating a substantial contribution of recycled oceanic crust in their mantle source. The spatial-temporal distribution and geochemical variations of the Leiqiong basalts highlight distinct roles of the plume and continental lithosphere during plume head emplacement. The lithosphere architecture strongly controls the ascent and emplacement of the plume head because the plume lacks sufficient vigor, owing to the impediment of significant stagnant slabs in the mantle transition zone beneath the Leiqiong area. Thus, the Hainan mantle plume initially ascended through a lithospheric weak zone in eastern Leiqiong area and subsequently propagated laterally across the northern Leiqiong area due to lateral variations in lithospheric architecture. The mantle sources of both stages were influenced by the Hainan plume during its ascent and lateral propagation. As the plume expanded, fusible recycled sedimentary components accumulated beneath the lithospheric weak zone, while the surrounding mantle was depleted due to tectonic suction toward the central region. Consequently, late-stage basalts in northwestern Leiqiong primarily record a signature of recycled oceanic crust introduced by the plume. Therefore, the plume governs the compositional evolution of the mantle sources during its propagation.

item.title: Geochemistry of magnetite and pyrite and implications for cobalt enrichment in the Jinshandian skarn Fe deposit, Edong, China
item.dc:creator: Xiaojun Zhang
Luo Heng
Wei Zhang
item.description: Understanding Co mobility and fluid evolution in skarn Fe systems requires integrated mineral-scale observations, yet such constraints remain limited for the Jinshandian deposit. This study combines field observations, petrography, and in-situ trace-element analyses of magnetite and pyrite to characterize hydrothermal processes and Co enrichment at Jinshandian. Magnetite occurs as endoskarn Mt1 within the intrusion and as exoskarn Mt2 in retrograde skarn, breccias, and massive magnetite ores. Mt1 and Mt2 show distinct trace-element signatures, including decreasing Ti, V, and Al and increasing Zn/V, Sn/Ga, and Co/Ni ratios from the intrusion outward, recording progressively intensified fluid–rock interaction. Pyrite occurs in multiple generations and displays strong Co zoning, with Co contents increasing from early Py1 to vein-type Py3b and is also high values in breccia-hosted Py2. These patterns are consistent with evolving fluid chemistry, enhanced rock buffering, evaporite involvement, and episodic depressurization during ore formation. Collectively, the magnetite–pyrite geochemistry records the hydrothermal evolution of the Jinshandian skarn Fe system and provides insights into the main controls on Co enrichment. Published: 2026-06-06T03:57:19+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geochemical Exploration Score: 0.565 (consider)

Understanding Co mobility and fluid evolution in skarn Fe systems requires integrated mineral-scale observations, yet such constraints remain limited for the Jinshandian deposit. This study combines field observations, petrography, and in-situ trace-element analyses of magnetite and pyrite to characterize hydrothermal processes and Co enrichment at Jinshandian. Magnetite occurs as endoskarn Mt1 within the intrusion and as exoskarn Mt2 in retrograde skarn, breccias, and massive magnetite ores. Mt1 and Mt2 show distinct trace-element signatures, including decreasing Ti, V, and Al and increasing Zn/V, Sn/Ga, and Co/Ni ratios from the intrusion outward, recording progressively intensified fluid–rock interaction. Pyrite occurs in multiple generations and displays strong Co zoning, with Co contents increasing from early Py1 to vein-type Py3b and is also high values in breccia-hosted Py2. These patterns are consistent with evolving fluid chemistry, enhanced rock buffering, evaporite involvement, and episodic depressurization during ore formation. Collectively, the magnetite–pyrite geochemistry records the hydrothermal evolution of the Jinshandian skarn Fe system and provides insights into the main controls on Co enrichment.

item.title: Coupled Cs–Cl partitioning in granitic melt–fluid systems: implications for Cs fertility and magma differentiation
item.dc:creator: Jianping Li
Xueyun Ma
Bing Xiao
item.description: Cesium is a critical metal commonly enriched in highly fractionated granite–pegmatite systems. Cesium concentrations in fluid inclusions are widely used to assess magma differentiation and Cs fertility in magmatic–hydrothermal systems, yet the enrichment behavior of Cs during progressive crystallization and fluid exsolution remains incompletely understood. Here, we combined fluid–melt partitioning experiments with Rayleigh-type modeling to investigate the coupled behavior of Cs and Cl during progressive magma crystallization and fluid exsolution. The experiments were conducted at 850 °C and 200 MPa using near-metaluminous to strongly peraluminous granitic melts and Cl-bearing aqueous fluids with chlorinities of ∼ 1–4 mol/kg H2O. Experimental results show that Cs partitioning is controlled primarily by fluid chlorinity rather than by the alumina saturation index (ASI) of the melt. Chlorine partitioning also increases strongly with fluid chlorinity, although melt ASI exerts a secondary effect. Cesium remains preferentially in the melt at low chlorinity but becomes increasingly fluid-compatible above ∼ 2 mol/kg H2O, consistent with transport of Cs predominantly as chloride complexes, most likely CsCl0 in the fluid. Rayleigh-type modeling shows that exsolved-fluid chlorinity rapidly decreases toward a lower, nearly stable range near ∼ 1 mol/kg H2O, limiting the duration of efficient Cs extraction. Under the modeled upper-crustal conditions, fluids extract less than ∼ 10% of the total magmatic Cs budget, indicating that fluid exsolution has limited capacity to generate bulk Cs enrichment. Among fluid parameters, fluid Cs/Rb may provide a useful complementary proxy of magma differentiation, but it is not fully independent of salinity and should therefore be applied with caution during the earliest high-salinity stage of fluid exsolution. Published: 2026-06-03T16:14:50+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.565 (consider)

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

Relevance: 0.565 (consider)

Cesium is a critical metal commonly enriched in highly fractionated granite–pegmatite systems. Cesium concentrations in fluid inclusions are widely used to assess magma differentiation and Cs fertility in magmatic–hydrothermal systems, yet the enrichment behavior of Cs during progressive crystallization and fluid exsolution remains incompletely understood. Here, we combined fluid–melt partitioning experiments with Rayleigh-type modeling to investigate the coupled behavior of Cs and Cl during progressive magma crystallization and fluid exsolution. The experiments were conducted at 850 °C and 200 MPa using near-metaluminous to strongly peraluminous granitic melts and Cl-bearing aqueous fluids with chlorinities of ∼ 1–4 mol/kg H2O. Experimental results show that Cs partitioning is controlled primarily by fluid chlorinity rather than by the alumina saturation index (ASI) of the melt. Chlorine partitioning also increases strongly with fluid chlorinity, although melt ASI exerts a secondary effect. Cesium remains preferentially in the melt at low chlorinity but becomes increasingly fluid-compatible above ∼ 2 mol/kg H2O, consistent with transport of Cs predominantly as chloride complexes, most likely CsCl0 in the fluid. Rayleigh-type modeling shows that exsolved-fluid chlorinity rapidly decreases toward a lower, nearly stable range near ∼ 1 mol/kg H2O, limiting the duration of efficient Cs extraction. Under the modeled upper-crustal conditions, fluids extract less than ∼ 10% of the total magmatic Cs budget, indicating that fluid exsolution has limited capacity to generate bulk Cs enrichment. Among fluid parameters, fluid Cs/Rb may provide a useful complementary proxy of magma differentiation, but it is not fully independent of salinity and should therefore be applied with caution during the earliest high-salinity stage of fluid exsolution.

item.title: Eastward Growth of the Tibetan Plateau Linked to Cenozoic Orogenic Wedge Deformation and Fluctuating Melt Sources
item.dc:creator: Quan Ou
Jia‐Jing Mei
Derek A. Wyman
Jian‐Qing Lai
Feng Zi
Kun Wang
Jing‐Yi Liu
Jia Liao
Zhiwu Li
item.description: Abstract The India–Asia collision continuously loads the Tibetan Plateau (TP), driving its Cenozoic eastward growth. This process has caused extensive crustal deformation and a diverse magmatic response. Here, we report on newly identified 35−6.5 Ma granitoids from Kangding area in southeastern (SE) TP. Based on elemental and isotopic data obtained from this study, including the first silicon isotope study of the Kangding Cenozoic granites, and data from the literature, the samples can be divided into two subgroups. Subgroup 1 (Sg1) has diagnostic geochemical characteristics (lower MgO, εNd(t), εHf(t)‐zircon, and higher K 2 O/Na 2 O, sum of light rare earth elements, Th, Th/U, Rb/Sr, ( 87 Sr/ 86 Sr) i totally) compared with Sg2. Sg1 also shows a strong correlation ( R 2 = 0.82) between δ 30 Si and Al/Si, while Sg2 does not. The features of Sg1 are best explained by a significant contribution from metasediments. Based on the restricted ages of the Sg1 magmas, we identify a temporary shift in the melt source during the early Miocene (ca. 20–10 Ma). At that time, the source involved the addition of metasedimentary materials, in contrast to the sediment‐poor source during the rest of the Cenozoic. Based on previous geophysical and regional deformation observations, the growth of the TP involved a gradual accentuation of imbrication structures. This was followed by the formation of a stress overload zone and the involvement of a crustal sedimentary source. Lastly, stress unloading formed an asymmetric topography across the SE plateau margin. Published: 2026-06-03T09:51:31+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.565 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Quan Ou; Jia‐Jing Mei; Derek A. Wyman; Jian‐Qing Lai; Feng Zi; Kun Wang; Jing‐Yi Liu; Jia Liao; Zhiwu Li

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2025gc012820

Relevance: 0.565 (consider)

Abstract The India–Asia collision continuously loads the Tibetan Plateau (TP), driving its Cenozoic eastward growth. This process has caused extensive crustal deformation and a diverse magmatic response. Here, we report on newly identified 35−6.5 Ma granitoids from Kangding area in southeastern (SE) TP. Based on elemental and isotopic data obtained from this study, including the first silicon isotope study of the Kangding Cenozoic granites, and data from the literature, the samples can be divided into two subgroups. Subgroup 1 (Sg1) has diagnostic geochemical characteristics (lower MgO, εNd(t), εHf(t)‐zircon, and higher K 2 O/Na 2 O, sum of light rare earth elements, Th, Th/U, Rb/Sr, ( 87 Sr/ 86 Sr) i totally) compared with Sg2. Sg1 also shows a strong correlation ( R 2 = 0.82) between δ 30 Si and Al/Si, while Sg2 does not. The features of Sg1 are best explained by a significant contribution from metasediments. Based on the restricted ages of the Sg1 magmas, we identify a temporary shift in the melt source during the early Miocene (ca. 20–10 Ma). At that time, the source involved the addition of metasedimentary materials, in contrast to the sediment‐poor source during the rest of the Cenozoic. Based on previous geophysical and regional deformation observations, the growth of the TP involved a gradual accentuation of imbrication structures. This was followed by the formation of a stress overload zone and the involvement of a crustal sedimentary source. Lastly, stress unloading formed an asymmetric topography across the SE plateau margin.

item.title: Transformation of Refractory Oceanic Lithospheric Mantle by Reactive Melt Infiltration: An Experimental Study on the Roles of Temperature, Melt Volume and Ascent Velocity
item.dc:creator: Yong‐Sheng Hou
Hong‐Yan Li
Chao Zhang
Yu Wang
Yi‐Gang Xu
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
item.description: Abstract Reactive melt infiltration critically modifies the physical and chemical properties of the oceanic lithospheric mantle (OLM). This process, involving melt‐rock reactions and in situ crystallization, exhibits substantial spatial and temporal variability driven by melt volume and ascent velocity. To explore how eclogite/pyroxenite‐derived plume melts transform refractory OLM, we conducted coupled reaction and in situ crystallization experiments at 2 GPa, systematically varying temperature, melt/rock ratio, and duration. We demonstrate that higher temperatures, lower melt/rock ratios, and shorter durations promote only orthopyroxene precipitation coupled with olivine dissolution, thereby preserving the starting harzburgitic lithology. In contrast, lower temperatures, higher melt/rock ratios, and longer durations facilitate clinopyroxene and orthopyroxene crystallization, converting refractory OLM harzburgite into fertile lherzolite or localized websterite. Beyond the well‐established influences of pressure and temperature, our study underscores the pivotal roles of melt volume and ascent velocity in governing metasomatism. This study reveals that clinopyroxene formed by high‐pressure (2 GPa) reactive melt infiltration explains clinopyroxene compositions in ocean island basalt–hosted mantle xenoliths, including high Na 2 O and TiO 2 and low Mg# and Cr#. Furthermore, these mineralogical transformations significantly alter OLM geophysical properties, reducing shear‐wave velocity by ∼2.2%–5.4% and increasing density by ∼1.4%–2.2%. We therefore propose that reactive melt infiltration explains shallow (50–60 km depth) low‐velocity anomalies observed in mature oceanic lithosphere (90–110 km thick) above a mantle plume. Concurrently, the associated density increase may induce delamination of metasomatized OLM, accompanied by buoyant ascent of underlying high‐temperature melting residues, ultimately driving vertical mantle convection within the plume. Published: 2026-06-05T06:25:25+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth Score: 0.564 (consider)

Authors: Yong‐Sheng Hou; Hong‐Yan Li; Chao Zhang; Yu Wang; Yi‐Gang Xu

Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth

DOI: 10.1029/2026jb033875

Relevance: 0.564 (consider)

Abstract Reactive melt infiltration critically modifies the physical and chemical properties of the oceanic lithospheric mantle (OLM). This process, involving melt‐rock reactions and in situ crystallization, exhibits substantial spatial and temporal variability driven by melt volume and ascent velocity. To explore how eclogite/pyroxenite‐derived plume melts transform refractory OLM, we conducted coupled reaction and in situ crystallization experiments at 2 GPa, systematically varying temperature, melt/rock ratio, and duration. We demonstrate that higher temperatures, lower melt/rock ratios, and shorter durations promote only orthopyroxene precipitation coupled with olivine dissolution, thereby preserving the starting harzburgitic lithology. In contrast, lower temperatures, higher melt/rock ratios, and longer durations facilitate clinopyroxene and orthopyroxene crystallization, converting refractory OLM harzburgite into fertile lherzolite or localized websterite. Beyond the well‐established influences of pressure and temperature, our study underscores the pivotal roles of melt volume and ascent velocity in governing metasomatism. This study reveals that clinopyroxene formed by high‐pressure (2 GPa) reactive melt infiltration explains clinopyroxene compositions in ocean island basalt–hosted mantle xenoliths, including high Na 2 O and TiO 2 and low Mg# and Cr#. Furthermore, these mineralogical transformations significantly alter OLM geophysical properties, reducing shear‐wave velocity by ∼2.2%–5.4% and increasing density by ∼1.4%–2.2%. We therefore propose that reactive melt infiltration explains shallow (50–60 km depth) low‐velocity anomalies observed in mature oceanic lithosphere (90–110 km thick) above a mantle plume. Concurrently, the associated density increase may induce delamination of metasomatized OLM, accompanied by buoyant ascent of underlying high‐temperature melting residues, ultimately driving vertical mantle convection within the plume.

item.title: Tungsten‐Tin Mineralization and Its Dynamic Background in the Bozhushan Ore District, Southeastern Yunnan: Constraints From Petrology, Geochronology and Geochemistry
item.dc:creator: Bode Lu
Xuelong Liu
Shitao Zhang
Jialong Cheng
Xianchao Chen
Jiehu Zhou
Xue Mi
Tao Wang
Guangzhi Meng
item.description: ABSTRACT The W–Sn–Ag–Pb–Zn polymetallic deposit in the Bozhushan ore concentration area is closely related to Late Cretaceous magmatic activity, and the diagenetic age of the granite body is 88.8–87.1 Ma. The granites in the Bozhushan ore district are characterized by high silica and aluminium contents, high abundances of Rb, Sr, Sn and Th, and low contents of Ba, P and Ti. The granites are peraluminous S‐type granites that underwent fractional crystallization during magmatic evolution. The ε Hf ( t ) values of the zircons from −17.92 to −1.77, with T DM2 age ranging from 1.1 to 2.0 Ga, indicate that they may be mainly derived from partial melting of the lower crust without the addition of mantle‐derived materials. The Late Cretaceous magmatic activity in the Bozhushan area formed in a syn‐collisional tectonic environment and transitioned to a post‐collisional orogenic environment. In the later stage, it transforms into a post‐collisional extensional environment. The upwelling of deep‐seated asthenospheric melts has provided substantial fluxes of W, Sn, Pb, Zn and other ore‐forming elements. The ore‐forming fluid and materials carried by magma rise and migrate along favourable parts of the structure to precipitate and mineralize. Therefore, understanding the tungsten‐tin metallogenic history in the Bozhushan ore district is important. Published: 2026-06-04T18:45:01+00:00 Venue: Acta Geologica Sinica - English Edition Score: 0.563 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Bode Lu; Xuelong Liu; Shitao Zhang; Jialong Cheng; Xianchao Chen; Jiehu Zhou; Xue Mi; Tao Wang; Guangzhi Meng

Journal: Acta Geologica Sinica - English Edition

DOI: 10.1111/1755-6724.70071

Relevance: 0.563 (consider)

ABSTRACT The W–Sn–Ag–Pb–Zn polymetallic deposit in the Bozhushan ore concentration area is closely related to Late Cretaceous magmatic activity, and the diagenetic age of the granite body is 88.8–87.1 Ma. The granites in the Bozhushan ore district are characterized by high silica and aluminium contents, high abundances of Rb, Sr, Sn and Th, and low contents of Ba, P and Ti. The granites are peraluminous S‐type granites that underwent fractional crystallization during magmatic evolution. The ε Hf ( t ) values of the zircons from −17.92 to −1.77, with T DM2 age ranging from 1.1 to 2.0 Ga, indicate that they may be mainly derived from partial melting of the lower crust without the addition of mantle‐derived materials. The Late Cretaceous magmatic activity in the Bozhushan area formed in a syn‐collisional tectonic environment and transitioned to a post‐collisional orogenic environment. In the later stage, it transforms into a post‐collisional extensional environment. The upwelling of deep‐seated asthenospheric melts has provided substantial fluxes of W, Sn, Pb, Zn and other ore‐forming elements. The ore‐forming fluid and materials carried by magma rise and migrate along favourable parts of the structure to precipitate and mineralize. Therefore, understanding the tungsten‐tin metallogenic history in the Bozhushan ore district is important.

item.title: New Insights Into Hikurangi Subduction Inputs and Megathrust Host Rocks Spanning Along‐Margin Changes in Fault Slip Behavior
item.dc:creator: Philip M. Barnes
Gareth J. Crutchley
Martin P. Crundwell
Stuart A. Henrys
Andrew Gase
Sam R. Davidson
Nathan Bangs
Harm Van Avendonk
Michael Underwood
Anthony E. Shorrock
Laura S. McDonald
Lorna J. Strachan
Helen C. Bostock
item.description: Abstract The seismic behavior of subduction megathrusts varies spatially and is influenced by the properties of subducting plates, including their sedimentary cover. Characterizing these subduction inputs is essential for understanding the mechanisms behind fault slip variability. The Hikurangi subduction margin exhibits well‐documented variations in fault slip ranging from large earthquakes to slow slip events and aseismic creeping. The variations coincide with along‐margin changes in convergence rate, trench sediment thickness, and accretionary wedge development. Here, we integrate regional seismic reflection profiles with drill cores from the International Ocean Discovery Program Expeditions 372 and 375, and with other seafloor samples. We find that the Hikurangi Trough hosts some of Earth's thickest trench sediments, at up to 10 km, including pelagic sediments and a >6 km thick siliciclastic trench wedge that is younger than previously thought. Seismic imaging reveals that the shallow megathrust depth increases by 3.5–4.5 km southward along the margin in parallel with increasing trench sediment thickness despite a “step up” of the fault's stratigraphic position to a different assemblage of host rocks. In contrast to the irregular northern megathrust associated with subducting seamounts and heterogeneous volcaniclastic and pelagic host rocks, the southern megathrust is smooth, hosted in calcareous pelagic rocks, and straddles inherited polygonal faults. These along‐strike variations in physical properties and megathrust geometry correspond to observed changes in fault slip behavior. Our findings provide new insights into the factors controlling fault slip variability, with implications for seismic hazard assessment at subduction zones worldwide and for understanding subduction margin evolution. Published: 2026-06-08T13:51:48+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.563 (consider)
