Authors: Philip M. Barnes; Gareth J. Crutchley; Martin P. Crundwell; Stuart A. Henrys; Andrew Gase; Sam R. Davidson; Nathan Bangs; Harm Van Avendonk; Michael Underwood; Anthony E. Shorrock; Laura S. McDonald; Lorna J. Strachan; Helen C. Bostock

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2025gc012734

Relevance: 0.563 (consider)

Abstract The seismic behavior of subduction megathrusts varies spatially and is influenced by the properties of subducting plates, including their sedimentary cover. Characterizing these subduction inputs is essential for understanding the mechanisms behind fault slip variability. The Hikurangi subduction margin exhibits well‐documented variations in fault slip ranging from large earthquakes to slow slip events and aseismic creeping. The variations coincide with along‐margin changes in convergence rate, trench sediment thickness, and accretionary wedge development. Here, we integrate regional seismic reflection profiles with drill cores from the International Ocean Discovery Program Expeditions 372 and 375, and with other seafloor samples. We find that the Hikurangi Trough hosts some of Earth's thickest trench sediments, at up to 10 km, including pelagic sediments and a >6 km thick siliciclastic trench wedge that is younger than previously thought. Seismic imaging reveals that the shallow megathrust depth increases by 3.5–4.5 km southward along the margin in parallel with increasing trench sediment thickness despite a “step up” of the fault's stratigraphic position to a different assemblage of host rocks. In contrast to the irregular northern megathrust associated with subducting seamounts and heterogeneous volcaniclastic and pelagic host rocks, the southern megathrust is smooth, hosted in calcareous pelagic rocks, and straddles inherited polygonal faults. These along‐strike variations in physical properties and megathrust geometry correspond to observed changes in fault slip behavior. Our findings provide new insights into the factors controlling fault slip variability, with implications for seismic hazard assessment at subduction zones worldwide and for understanding subduction margin evolution.

item.title: Upper crustal velocity imaging and deep structural mechanisms of the Tethyan Himalayan gneiss domes
item.dc:creator: Guangwen Wang
Zhanwu Lu
Wenhui Li
Shuai Xue
Yao Liang
Wei Cai
Si Chen
Guowei Wu
item.description: The Tethyan Himalayan tectonic belt, which is located at the forefront of the Indo-Eurasian continental collision, has various extensional detachment structures, including gneiss domes, the South Tibetan detachment system (STDS), and north-south−trending rifts (NSTRs). Gneiss domes are tectonic features of the Tethyan Himalayan belt. Previous studies have revealed significant differences in the formation mechanisms and mineral enrichment patterns of North Himalayan gneiss domes, suggesting that their formation was affected by multiple tectonic processes, such as magmatic activity and extensional detachment. However, the mechanisms responsible for these differences and the relationship between shallow and deep structures remain poorly understood. In this study, we used short-period seismic array data from the eastern and western segments of the Tethyan Himalayan belt for analysis. We applied ambient noise tomography and incorporated constraints from deep seismic reflection data to conduct upper crustal imaging of the gneiss domes across the eastern and western regions. Our findings indicate the following: (1) distinct seismic velocity patterns reveal low-velocity channels beneath the Cuonadong (CND) and Malashan (MLS) domes, contrasting with the high-velocity anomalies under the Yalaxiangbo (YLXB) dome, suggesting fundamental differences in their formation mechanisms; (2) the NSTRs and STDS both contributed to dome formation, but the former had a greater effect on the CND and MLS domes, whereas the latter played a dominant role in the evolution of the YLXB dome; and (3) the formation of these domes was followed by magmatic differentiation and crystallization, which concurrently controlled rare metal mineralization. Spatial imaging of the deep structures of leucogranites provides critical insights into how the unique upper crustal architecture of different domes influences rare metal enrichment patterns. Published: 2026-06-02T19:01:08+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.559 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Guangwen Wang; Zhanwu Lu; Wenhui Li; Shuai Xue; Yao Liang; Wei Cai; Si Chen; Guowei Wu

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b38843.1

Relevance: 0.559 (consider)

The Tethyan Himalayan tectonic belt, which is located at the forefront of the Indo-Eurasian continental collision, has various extensional detachment structures, including gneiss domes, the South Tibetan detachment system (STDS), and north-south−trending rifts (NSTRs). Gneiss domes are tectonic features of the Tethyan Himalayan belt. Previous studies have revealed significant differences in the formation mechanisms and mineral enrichment patterns of North Himalayan gneiss domes, suggesting that their formation was affected by multiple tectonic processes, such as magmatic activity and extensional detachment. However, the mechanisms responsible for these differences and the relationship between shallow and deep structures remain poorly understood. In this study, we used short-period seismic array data from the eastern and western segments of the Tethyan Himalayan belt for analysis. We applied ambient noise tomography and incorporated constraints from deep seismic reflection data to conduct upper crustal imaging of the gneiss domes across the eastern and western regions. Our findings indicate the following: (1) distinct seismic velocity patterns reveal low-velocity channels beneath the Cuonadong (CND) and Malashan (MLS) domes, contrasting with the high-velocity anomalies under the Yalaxiangbo (YLXB) dome, suggesting fundamental differences in their formation mechanisms; (2) the NSTRs and STDS both contributed to dome formation, but the former had a greater effect on the CND and MLS domes, whereas the latter played a dominant role in the evolution of the YLXB dome; and (3) the formation of these domes was followed by magmatic differentiation and crystallization, which concurrently controlled rare metal mineralization. Spatial imaging of the deep structures of leucogranites provides critical insights into how the unique upper crustal architecture of different domes influences rare metal enrichment patterns.

item.title: Genesis of the Kuogeshaye gold deposit, Western Junggar, NW China: Evidence from fluid inclusions, stable isotopes, and mineralogy
item.dc:creator: Qihui Shi
Yongmei Zhang
Xuexiang Gu
Kun Wang
Shuo Gao
Fenghe Tian
item.description: The Kuogeshaye gold deposit, located in the southern part of the Western Junggar region, is hosted in Carboniferous volcanic-sedimentary rocks and diorite-granodiorite dikes. Its genesis remains controversial because the sources of ore-forming fluids and mineralization processes are poorly constrained. This study provides new insights into the genesis of the gold deposit and offers guidance for further exploration through investigations of mineralization and alteration characteristics, fluid inclusions, trace-element compositions of quartz, and H-O-C-S isotopes. Ore bodies occur mainly as quartz-sulfide veins controlled by NE- and NNE-trending faults, and the principal ore minerals include arsenopyrite, pyrite, stibnite, native gold, and minor Sb-bearing minerals. Alteration shows zonation from silicification and beresitization to chloritization. Two stages are defined: (Ⅰ) quartz-sulfide and (Ⅱ) quartz-calcite. Fluid inclusions indicate stage Ⅰ formed at 162–286 °C with salinities of 2.2–6.6 wt% NaCl equivalent, whereas stage II formed at lower temperatures and salinities. Isotopic data (δ 18O = 5.3–7.4 ‰, δD = –110.9 to –115.7 ‰, δ 34S = –1.5 to 2.9 ‰) and δ 13C values (–7.8 to –6.7 ‰) indicate the ore-forming fluids were dominantly magmatic in origin, with meteoric water involvement during the late stage. Quartz chemistry shows enrichment in Al and Li but depletion in Ti, Ge, and Sb. These results suggest that the deposit belongs to a medium- to low-temperature magmatic-hydrothermal vein-type gold system, in which fluid boiling triggered gold precipitation. Favorable exploration targets include fault intersections and zones with dense intermediate to acidic dikes, while deeper levels may host porphyry-type mineralization. Published: 2026-06-03T00:11:38+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.558 (consider)

Relevance: 0.558 (consider)

The Kuogeshaye gold deposit, located in the southern part of the Western Junggar region, is hosted in Carboniferous volcanic-sedimentary rocks and diorite-granodiorite dikes. Its genesis remains controversial because the sources of ore-forming fluids and mineralization processes are poorly constrained. This study provides new insights into the genesis of the gold deposit and offers guidance for further exploration through investigations of mineralization and alteration characteristics, fluid inclusions, trace-element compositions of quartz, and H-O-C-S isotopes. Ore bodies occur mainly as quartz-sulfide veins controlled by NE- and NNE-trending faults, and the principal ore minerals include arsenopyrite, pyrite, stibnite, native gold, and minor Sb-bearing minerals. Alteration shows zonation from silicification and beresitization to chloritization. Two stages are defined: (Ⅰ) quartz-sulfide and (Ⅱ) quartz-calcite. Fluid inclusions indicate stage Ⅰ formed at 162–286 °C with salinities of 2.2–6.6 wt% NaCl equivalent, whereas stage II formed at lower temperatures and salinities. Isotopic data (δ 18O = 5.3–7.4 ‰, δD = –110.9 to –115.7 ‰, δ 34S = –1.5 to 2.9 ‰) and δ 13C values (–7.8 to –6.7 ‰) indicate the ore-forming fluids were dominantly magmatic in origin, with meteoric water involvement during the late stage. Quartz chemistry shows enrichment in Al and Li but depletion in Ti, Ge, and Sb. These results suggest that the deposit belongs to a medium- to low-temperature magmatic-hydrothermal vein-type gold system, in which fluid boiling triggered gold precipitation. Favorable exploration targets include fault intersections and zones with dense intermediate to acidic dikes, while deeper levels may host porphyry-type mineralization.

item.title: Ti-poor high-Al and high-Cr podiform chromitites in the Ngape ophiolite, myanmar: constraints from mineral chemistry and Re-Os isotopes
item.dc:creator: Toe Toe Kyaw Lin
Hong-Fu Zhang
A-Bing Lin
Juan Zhang
item.description: The Ngape ophiolite in western Myanmar preserves critical records of Neo-Tethyan Ocean tectonic evolution. Here, we present the integrated mineral chemistry, whole-rock platinum group element (PGE), and Re-Os isotopic study of podiform chromitites from this ophiolite to constrain their petrogenesis and tectonic setting. The chromitites are classified into high-Al (Cr# = 56.06–60.06) and high-Cr (Cr# = 63.01–72.63) types. Both types are notably poor in TiO2 (<0.29 wt%). Calculated parental melts indicate that the high-Al chromitites crystallized from the tholeiitic melts, whereas the high-Cr chromitites originated from boninitic melts. The chromitites exhibit enrichment of IPGE (Os, Ir, Ru) over PPGE (Pt, Pd, Rh), and possess unradiogenic, homogeneous 187Os/188Os ratios (0.12638–0.12863; average 0.12695). These PGE and Os isotopic signatures point to a depleted mantle source. Trace elements (e.g., V, Sc, Ga) further reveal that the high-Cr chromitites formed under higher oxygen fugacity and from a more depleted source than the high-Al variety. The coexistence of these Ti-poor chromitites records a progressive magmatic evolution within a forearc mantle wedge, transitioning from nascent subduction initiation (high-Al type) to a geochemically mature stage of the subduction initiation sequence (high-Cr type). This study provides the comprehensive Re-Os isotopic dataset for Myanmar ophiolites and documents a complete, in-situ magmatic progression during forearc evolution in the Neo-Tethyan realm. Published: 2026-06-07T14:26:51+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.557 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Toe Toe Kyaw Lin; Hong-Fu Zhang; A-Bing Lin; Juan Zhang

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107358

Relevance: 0.557 (consider)

The Ngape ophiolite in western Myanmar preserves critical records of Neo-Tethyan Ocean tectonic evolution. Here, we present the integrated mineral chemistry, whole-rock platinum group element (PGE), and Re-Os isotopic study of podiform chromitites from this ophiolite to constrain their petrogenesis and tectonic setting. The chromitites are classified into high-Al (Cr# = 56.06–60.06) and high-Cr (Cr# = 63.01–72.63) types. Both types are notably poor in TiO2 (<0.29 wt%). Calculated parental melts indicate that the high-Al chromitites crystallized from the tholeiitic melts, whereas the high-Cr chromitites originated from boninitic melts. The chromitites exhibit enrichment of IPGE (Os, Ir, Ru) over PPGE (Pt, Pd, Rh), and possess unradiogenic, homogeneous 187Os/188Os ratios (0.12638–0.12863; average 0.12695). These PGE and Os isotopic signatures point to a depleted mantle source. Trace elements (e.g., V, Sc, Ga) further reveal that the high-Cr chromitites formed under higher oxygen fugacity and from a more depleted source than the high-Al variety. The coexistence of these Ti-poor chromitites records a progressive magmatic evolution within a forearc mantle wedge, transitioning from nascent subduction initiation (high-Al type) to a geochemically mature stage of the subduction initiation sequence (high-Cr type). This study provides the comprehensive Re-Os isotopic dataset for Myanmar ophiolites and documents a complete, in-situ magmatic progression during forearc evolution in the Neo-Tethyan realm.

item.title: Along‐Strike Seismic Structure of the Andreanof Aleutian Arc Segment and Implications for the Formation of Continental Crust
item.dc:creator: Hannah F. Mark
Daniel Lizarralde
Donna J. Shillington
Valeria Cortés‐Rivas
Mark D. Behn
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
item.description: Abstract Crust formed in volcanic arcs is accreted to continents over geologic time, serving as the source material for new continental crust in Earth's present and recent past. Geochemically, arcs are found to be significantly more mafic than bulk continental crust. Reconciling this compositional difference either requires that arc crust evolves to match the composition of continents, or suggests that the processes forming arcs in the present differ from those of the geologic past. We present a new along‐strike seismic model of an intact segment of the Aleutian Arc, and use thermodynamic models to show that its overall composition is mafic compared to the composition of bulk continental crust. Punctuated low velocity anomalies imaged in the lower crust beneath Atka and Tanaga are interpreted as more felsic and hotter than surrounding material, suggesting that volcanic centers within the segment are evolving at different rates. Published: 2026-06-02T02:14:27+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth Score: 0.555 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hannah F. Mark; Daniel Lizarralde; Donna J. Shillington; Valeria Cortés‐Rivas; Mark D. Behn

Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth

DOI: 10.1029/2025jb033339

Relevance: 0.555 (consider)

Abstract Crust formed in volcanic arcs is accreted to continents over geologic time, serving as the source material for new continental crust in Earth's present and recent past. Geochemically, arcs are found to be significantly more mafic than bulk continental crust. Reconciling this compositional difference either requires that arc crust evolves to match the composition of continents, or suggests that the processes forming arcs in the present differ from those of the geologic past. We present a new along‐strike seismic model of an intact segment of the Aleutian Arc, and use thermodynamic models to show that its overall composition is mafic compared to the composition of bulk continental crust. Punctuated low velocity anomalies imaged in the lower crust beneath Atka and Tanaga are interpreted as more felsic and hotter than surrounding material, suggesting that volcanic centers within the segment are evolving at different rates.
